stages.goal: Understand what DevForge is, what problem it solves, and whether it's worth 30 more minutes of evaluation time.
devforge_touchpoints: README.md — project mission, Quick Start, feature bullet list
PLAN.md — architecture rationale and design principles (contributor-oriented)
PyPI package metadata (description, classifiers)
friction_points: README.md is written entirely in Chinese — English-speaking developers cannot read the primary discovery document
No one-sentence English tagline on the repository or in pyproject.toml description that distinguishes DevForge from generic LangGraph frameworks
PLAN.md is contributor-oriented (explains phase deliverables) but not discoverer-oriented (doesn't explain the 'what and why' for a first-time reader)
No 'why not just use LangGraph directly?' explanation on the landing page
smooth_points: README.md Quick Start section exists with concrete CLI commands
Feature bullet list in README is concrete (lists orchestration capabilities)
Repository structure is clean and follows standard Python package layout

stages.goal: Verify that DevForge can run locally in under 5 minutes and produces observable output that matches the stated value proposition.
devforge_touchpoints: devforge fixture game_project — runs a complete cycle against a built-in fixture without any project setup
devforge fixture ecommerce_project --json — returns machine-readable cycle result
devforge doctor — checks executor availability immediately after install
uv sync --extra dev — single dependency install command
friction_points: devforge doctor requires codex or claude CLI to be installed and logged in — first-time evaluation in a clean environment shows all executors as 'missing' or 'blocked', which reads as failure even though stub mode works
fixture output is dense JSON with no human-readable summary mode by default — first-time users don't know what to look for
No README section explaining what 'stub transport submitted' in fixture output means (expected behavior, not an error)
smooth_points: run_fixture_cycle works entirely offline with stub transport — zero network requirements for evaluation
fixture command output includes cycle_id, selected_work_packages, dispatches — sufficient to validate the core dispatch loop
Two fixture archetypes available (game, ecommerce) covering different project types
test_graph_runner.py::test_run_cycle_selects_and_dispatches_fixture_work shows exactly what output to expect
evidence: src/devforge/main.py:84-153 — run_executor_doctor implementation with actionable messages

stages.goal: Bootstrap DevForge into a real project and understand how to configure the snapshot and project preferences.
devforge_touchpoints: devforge init — writes .devforge/devforge.snapshot.json and .devforge/devforge.project_config.json
devforge init --workspace — detects multi-project structure, runs topology classifier
devforge init --guided — forces wizard-mode preferences even in non-interactive terminal
Wizard presets: LLM_SETUP_PRESETS (auto/offline/live), KNOWLEDGE_SETUP_PRESETS (balanced/implementation/testing), PULL_SETUP_PRESETS (standard/lean/rich)
friction_points: After init, the generated snapshot JSON has no comments or embedded documentation — users don't know which fields are safe to edit manually
devforge init --workspace requires a running LLM (topology classifier) — fails without API keys even when the project structure is obvious
No documentation explaining the snapshot JSON schema — users can't craft a snapshot from scratch without reading state/ source code
The snapshot file name (devforge.snapshot.json) is hardcoded but can be changed — no documentation on when/why to use a different name
smooth_points: Wizard presets abstract llm_preferences/knowledge_preferences/pull_policy_overrides into three human-readable choices
init writes both snapshot and project_config in one step — no manual file creation needed
WORKSPACE_PROJECT_MARKERS covers all major build systems (pyproject.toml, package.json, go.mod, Cargo.toml, etc.)
maybe_apply_fixture_project_config auto-discovers sibling config files — zero-config for fixture runs

stages.goal: Run reliable development cycles against a real project, resume interrupted cycles, and tune executor routing behavior.
devforge_touchpoints: devforge snapshot .devforge/devforge.snapshot.json — execute a cycle against own project
devforge snapshot ... --persistence-root .devforge — enable durable cycle resume
devforge snapshot ... --project-config ... — apply per-project LLM/knowledge overrides
devforge repl — interactive session for conversational cycle continuation
run_snapshot_cycle() Python API for CI/scripting integration
friction_points: No documentation on how cycle resume works — users don't know that --persistence-root enables SQLite snapshot + event log
The snapshot JSON must be manually updated after each cycle (updated snapshot in result['snapshot']) — no automated write-back is documented
Work package status transitions (9 statuses) are not documented for users — when a cycle ends with some WPs in 'blocked' status, users don't know the expected next action
No built-in cycle visualization or summary beyond raw JSON output
smooth_points: run_snapshot_cycle signature is clean — one path param, optional persistence and config kwargs
Cycle result always includes updated snapshot dict — callers can write it back atomically
devforge repl provides conversational continuation for iterative cycles
project_config llm_preferences support per-task model routing (concept_model, planning_model, retry_model) — granular without being complex

stages.goal: Add a custom executor adapter, define custom knowledge packs, or tune context pull budgets — without modifying orchestration graph code.
devforge_touchpoints: ExecutorAdapter Protocol in executors/base.py — implement 11-method contract
BaseExecutorAdapter in executors/adapters.py — inherit and override CLI argv builder
register_executor_adapter() — register custom adapter at runtime
pull_policy_overrides in project_config.json — tune per-role context budgets
knowledge/ module — add custom KnowledgeDocument to KNOWLEDGE_REGISTRY
EXECUTOR_CAPABILITIES — register capability metadata separately from logic
friction_points: No 'Build Your First Executor Adapter' how-to guide — the Protocol definition alone is insufficient documentation
submit vs submit_request vs dispatch naming is ambiguous — no documented explanation of when to implement which method vs defer to base class
Custom knowledge packs require direct modification of KNOWLEDGE_REGISTRY — no plugin/config-based knowledge registration path
SubprocessTransport._run_subprocess_request internals are not documented — custom adapters needing custom transport logic must reverse-engineer the source
No test recipe for custom adapters — how to use MockLLMClient + fixture patterns for adapter unit testing is undocumented
smooth_points: BaseExecutorAdapter handles ~8 of 11 Protocol methods as stubs/defaults — minimum viable custom adapter is ~30 lines
SubprocessTransport submit/poll/cancel lifecycle is reusable for any subprocess-based tool
pull_policy_overrides is pure config — no code changes needed to tune context budgets
EXECUTOR_CAPABILITIES is separate from adapter logic — can define capability metadata without touching adapter code

stages.goal: Add new meta-graph nodes, extend RuntimeState, or add planning decision chains while keeping the test suite green and consistent with the orchestration contract.
devforge_touchpoints: src/devforge/graph/nodes.py — node function signatures and RuntimeState mutation contract
src/devforge/graph/runtime_state.py — RuntimeState typed dataclass (add new fields here)
src/devforge/graph/langgraph_builder.py — StateGraph wiring (add conditional edges)
src/devforge/graph/skeleton.py — META_GRAPH_NODES, NEXT_STEP_BY_EVENT registry
src/devforge/planning/ — decider function pattern (llm_*_decider)
src/devforge/llm/mock.py + fixture JSONs — test isolation without live LLM
256-test regression suite as contribution baseline
friction_points: No CONTRIBUTING.md or contributor guide — newcomers have no documented workflow
LangGraph StateGraph wiring in langgraph_builder.py is complex — no architectural explanation of why the meta-graph was designed as a directed graph vs a simple loop
PLAN.md is the closest thing to a contributor architecture guide but its phase deliverables are outdated (C002, C003)
next_step_for_state() transition logic in transitions.py is not documented — contributors must read code to understand node ordering rules
No documented convention for naming new nodes, new RuntimeState fields, or new planning deciders
smooth_points: RuntimeState is a typed dataclass — adding new fields is one line and immediately visible to IDE
MockLLMClient returns deterministic structured responses — full cycle tests run offline with predictable assertions
Planning module has consistent decider pattern (StructuredGenerationRequest/Response) — new deciders follow clear shape
256 tests serve as explicit regression baseline — contributors know immediately when they break existing behavior
Pure node functions (no class state) are composable without side effects
evidence: tests/test_graph_runner.py — regression tests with fixture + mock
